Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered most of the time. I tried to set aside specific hours, but with six online courses and limited resources at home, things got chaotic. I'm someone who learns best by doing things with my hands, so I'd often try to work through practice problems or physically write out notes rather than just reading. I'd usually end up studying in bursts whenever I could find quiet time, which wasn't always consistent.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on what was due. I'd have these intense periods right before deadlines where I'd cram everything in, then quieter weeks where I barely touched the material. With medium stress levels constantly hanging over me, I found it hard to maintain any real consistency. Some weeks I'd hit close to my 19 hours of studying, other weeks way less.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline approaching or seeing a notification that something new was posted. Sometimes my peer study group would message me saying we needed to check something on the LMS, and that would push me to log in. I wasn't someone who logged in just to browse around - there had to be a specific reason pulling me there.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. The LMS never felt like a natural part of my routine - it was more like a tool I used when necessary. The 32 times I logged in were mostly driven by specific needs rather than habit. I think because I prefer hands-on learning, sitting at a screen watching videos or clicking through modules just didn't stick as a daily thing for me.
Q5: The discussion forums were actually helpful because I could interact with other students and learn from their perspectives. I had about 47 peer interactions which helped me understand concepts better than just reading alone. When there were practical exercises or anything I could actually work through step by step, those felt most useful. The videos were okay but I'd watch maybe 20-something minutes and then need to do something more active to really absorb it.
Q6: I definitely ignored a lot of the longer reading materials and some of the supplementary videos. With limited internet access quality at home, streaming longer content was frustrating and ate up data. I'd often skip optional readings because I was already overwhelmed trying to keep up with the core requirements across all six courses. My assignment completion rate shows that - I just couldn't get to everything.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it directly connected to assignments or exams, or if it involved actually doing something rather than passively consuming information. When I could practice a skill or work through a problem, that felt productive. If something was just reading for reading's sake with no clear connection to what I'd be assessed on, my motivation to engage dropped significantly.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford most of the time. With my anxiety levels being what they are, I was always worried about the required stuff first. Required activities had this weight to them - I knew I had to do them or face consequences. Optional ones, even if interesting, got pushed aside because I was already struggling to complete the mandatory work at 38% completion.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download materials when I could and then work through them without being connected. I'd handwrite notes, create my own practice problems, and work through things physically because that's how I learn best. My textbooks got way more use than my LMS activity would suggest. I also studied with peers in person quite a bit.
Q10: There were many times I'd be sitting with printed notes or downloaded PDFs, working through problems by hand for hours, and none of that showed up anywhere. My peer study sessions where we'd meet up and work through difficult concepts together - that was some of my most productive learning, but the LMS had no idea. I'd also re-read my handwritten notes multiple times before exams.
Q11: Yeah, this was pretty common for me. I'd access a PDF or download lecture slides once, and then that material would live on my laptop or in printed form for weeks. I'd review it multiple times offline, especially before exams. Given my limited resources and sometimes spotty internet, downloading once and studying repeatedly offline just made more practical sense.
Q12: There were definitely harder periods, especially mid-semester when everything piled up across all six courses. My anxiety would spike and I'd feel paralyzed about where to even start. When stress hit medium-to-high levels, I'd sometimes just shut down and avoid the LMS entirely for a few days. Financial stress from having low resources also distracted me from focusing on coursework.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from juggling everything, I'd log into the LMS but not really absorb anything. Confusion about assignment requirements made me avoid starting things because I didn't want to do them wrong. Competing responsibilities - like needing to help out at home or deal with financial stuff - meant the LMS often got pushed to late night hours when I was already drained.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd open videos and let them play while my mind wandered somewhere else entirely. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really processing what people were saying. Sometimes I'd have the LMS open for an hour but accomplish almost nothing meaningful because my anxiety was making it impossible to concentrate.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. The anxiety made starting things feel overwhelming - like if I couldn't do it perfectly, why start at all? I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt better, but tomorrow came with its own problems. That's probably why my assignment completion was only 38% even though when I did submit, I scored really well.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled everything. I'd have almost no LMS activity for days, then suddenly log in multiple times as a deadline approached. It wasn't healthy, but the pressure of a deadline was sometimes the only thing that could cut through my anxiety and force me to act. My response time averaging over 11 hours shows I wasn't exactly prompt about things.
Q17: When I got good feedback, especially on assignments where I scored well, it motivated me to keep trying. But feedback often came too late to really change my approach for the next thing. The peer support intervention helped somewhat - having others check in on me made me feel less alone. Negative or confusing feedback sometimes made my anxiety worse and I'd avoid that course for a while.
Q18: The course structure with six online courses simultaneously was honestly overwhelming. Having everything online meant I had to be self-directed, which is hard when motivation is low. The structure didn't really accommodate my kinesthetic learning style - there wasn't much hands-on stuff built in. I had to create my own active learning opportunities outside the system.
Q19: The lack of interactive, hands-on components made engagement harder for someone like me who learns by doing. When courses had clear, broken-down modules with practical exercises, that helped. But when it was just walls of text or long videos, I'd disengage quickly. The discussion forums being active made things easier because at least there was some human interaction there.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me on my anxious days - logging in, clicking around, maybe watching part of a video, opening assignment instructions multiple times but never actually starting. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. Sometimes you're just circling around the work because you're too stressed or confused to actually dive in and complete things.
Q21: That resonates with me somewhat. My exam score was decent and I passed, even though my LMS engagement looked low. A lot of my actual learning happened offline - with downloaded materials, handwritten notes, peer study sessions. Someone could be genuinely learning and understanding the material through methods that don't leave digital footprints at all.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when someone logs in, how long they spend, what they click on. But they completely miss the quality of that time - whether someone is actually focused or just going through motions. They miss all offline studying, peer discussions, the mental processing that happens away from screens. They can't capture understanding or struggle.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and wrestling with the material, not just checking boxes. It's when I'm genuinely trying to understand something, asking questions, making connections to other things I know. For me, it often looks like sitting with physical notes, working through problems by hand, discussing concepts with classmates. It's mental investment, not just time spent logged in.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my anxiety spirals that kept me from starting work, or the peer support sessions that helped pull me through. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with printed materials or handwritten notes. It can't see that when I did engage deeply, I produced quality work - my assignment scores prove that. It misses the whole human struggle behind the data points.
